segments.s508-g185: بیاسایم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form meaning “may I rest” or “let me rest” (poetic/archaic). ایدر is an older form of این‌در/این‌جا meaning “here.” که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason (“because/that”) and is followed by the noun phrase چندین سپاه, where چندین = “several” modifies سپاه = “armies/host.”
segments.s508-g186: به تندی is an adverbial phrase: به + تندی = “with haste / hastily.” نشاید is an impersonal/archaic negative copular/auxiliary meaning “it is not fitting” or “one should not.” کشیدن is the infinitive “to go/march/proceed” here (to lead/move troops), and به راه literally “towards/on the road” completes the verbal phrase “کشیدن به راه” = “to take to the road / to march.” Word order places the adverbial before the impersonal predicate and the infinitive after it.
segments.s508-g187: فرستاده is the noun “messenger.” برگشت و آمد is a compound sequence of past verbs “returned and came,” giving a swift, narrative motion. چو is the simile marker “as/like,” and باد is the noun “wind,” so آمد چو باد = “he came like the wind” (quickly).
segments.s508-g188: به is the preposition “to/for” marking the indirect object (to the faghfur). فغفور is the title for the Byzantine ruler (the addressee). پیغام قیصر is a genitive group “Caesar’s message” (پیغام = message, قیصر = Caesar). بداد in context is the verb meaning “(he) gave / delivered,” but the provided senses list does not include that verbal meaning, so the correct sense is not among the supplied options.
segments.s508-g9: نوشتند is a past plural verb ('they wrote/wrote down') placed at the start in this epic style. هرگونه‌ای خوب و زشت is the object phrase ('every kind, good and bad'), with هرگونه‌ای = 'any/each kind' modified by the paired adjectives خوب و زشت.
segments.s508-g10: نویسنده ('the scribe/writer') is the agent; چون here means 'as/when' and introduces the circumstance; نامه اندر نوشت is a small clause 'he wrote the letter', with اندر = 'in/into' (poetic preposition) and نوشت a past singular 'wrote'.
segments.s508-g11: سکندر is the proper name; بشد is the past/ narrative form of شدن ('it happened / he became'); چون = 'when/as' introduces the time clause; فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a sent one' = 'a messenger' (formed from فرستاد, here with an indefinite/agentive -ای).
segments.s508-g12: گزین کرد is a compound verbal phrase ('he chose' — گزین + کرد = 'made a choice'); بینادل is an obscure/uncertain reading here (textual/rare word) and so its precise sense is unclear; آزاده‌ای is the descriptive indefinite 'a noble/free-born one' modifying the chosen messenger.
segments.s508-g13: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'who'); با او = 'with him'; the following بدی is problematic in the transmitted reading (it does not match the usual lexical senses listed), but syntactically it functions in this clause and is followed by qualities یک‌دل و یک‌سخن = 'single‑hearted and single‑spoken' (i.e. united in heart and speech).
segments.s508-g14: بگوید here is the reported/optative verb ('he should say' / 'let him say' / 'he may tell') introducing direct speech; به مهتر = 'to the chief/elder' is the indirect object; که introduces the quote or content 'that: کن یا مکن' = 'do or do not' (imperative/negative imperative). Note: the lexical senses list for بگوید in the data does not match the usual verb sense, so it is treated as the reported-speech verb in context.
segments.s508-g15: سپه را is the definite object with the enclitic را ('the army'); به سالار لشکر = 'to the commander of the host' (به + title + apposition); سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over' (سپردن) completing the clause (typical SOV order with object marked by را).
segments.s508-g16: وزان = 'and from that/from them' (connective + partitive); رومیان 'the Romans' is the source phrase; پنج دانا = 'five wise (men)' where the numeral precedes the adjective+noun group; ببرد is a past narrative form of بردن here meaning 'he took/carried off' (third-person past narrative in this context).
segments.s508-g81: زبانش = زبان + enclitic possessive ش (‘his/its’); the possessor attaches after the noun. به کردار is a classical simile/prepositional phrase meaning “in the manner of / like” (به + noun), followed by کردار (“manner/behavior”) specifying the comparison. برنده is an adjective ‘cutting/sharp’ modifying تیغ, so the whole segment reads literally “his tongue in the manner of a cutting sword.” Word order places the simile phrase between the possessor and the noun being compared.
segments.s508-g82: This clause supplies the action: به + چربی عقاب اندر آرد z میغ. به … اندر آرد is a directional construction: به (towards/into) + place (چربی عقاب) + اندر (in/within) + آرد (3rd‑person past ‘he/it brings/puts’). ز میغ is an ablative/poetic phrase ('from/of the blade') specifying instrument or source. Together it reads ‘(he) thrusts/puts into the eagle's fat (into it) from/with the blade.’
segments.s508-g83: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' بشنید is past tense (3rd person) 'heard.' فغفور چین is a title + place: 'faghfur (emperor) of China' — the title precedes the place name here. این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this speech' placed after the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s508-g84: یکی دیگر is an idiom meaning 'another / one other.' اندیشه افگند is a compact verbal phrase: اندیشه (thought/plan) + افگند (past 'cast/laid/formed'); here it means 'he formed/devized a thought/plan.' بن follows as nominal 'foundation/root,' used figuratively with افگند to mean 'laid down' or 'devised' — so the phrase means 'he cast/laid another plan' i.e. 'he conceived another plan.'
segments.s508-g85: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past form 'he commanded/said.' تا here introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that / and then'), linking to خوان و می‌خواستند: خوان (banquet/feast) is the object, و is the coordinating conjunction, and می‌خواستند is the imperfect/plural 'they wanted' or 'were about to'—the verb expresses their ensuing desire or intent to prepare the feast.
segments.s508-g86: به + باغ indicates direction/location ('to/in the garden'); اندر reiterates 'in/within' (classical poetic doubl‑ing of locative), ایوان is the place 'iwan/porch/veranda,' and بیاراستند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they adorned/arranged.' The verb follows the place expressions, a common poetic word order: 'they adorned the iwan in the garden.'
segments.s508-g87: همی is a continuous/progressive adverb 'kept/was continually.' خورد is a past form 'ate/drank' (here context shows 'drank'). می‌تا is the temporal 'until.' جهان تیره شد is a subordinate clause 'the world became dark' with شد as the past of 'to become.' The verb خورد appears before the temporal clause — a common poetic ordering expressing 'they continued to drink until...'.
segments.s508-g88: سر میگساران is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the heads of the wine‑drinkers' (سر = head). ز introduces cause/agent here ('from/because of'), می‌خیره شد is a stative/resultative 'became transfixed/was stupefied' (3rd‑person past), so the clause means 'the drinkers' heads were transfixed because of the wine' (i.e. they were dazed by drinking).
segments.s508-g89: سپهدار چین is a noun phrase 'the commander (سپهدار) of China (چین)' with the possessor following the head (postpositive). با + person + گفت functions here as 'said to (the) envoy' — با marks the recipient and گفت is the past tense 3rd singular 'he said'.
segments.s508-g90: که introduces reported content ('that'). با شاه تو = 'with your king' (شاه with the enclitic/possessive pronoun تو after it). مشتری is the planet Jupiter as the semantic subject; باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'; جفت is the predicate 'paired/matched' — the clause is a nominal clause with باد giving the wish‑like force.
segments.s508-g91: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. روشن شود is a passive/impersonal-style construction 'becomes clear' (شود = 3rd‑person form of شدن used here as a present/future marker). نامه is the object/topic 'the letter', and پاسخ کنیم uses the subjunctive/volitional کنیم (1st plural) to express 'we will reply' or 'we shall answer'.
segments.s508-g92: به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'for/for the meeting with you' (تو = 'you', possessive/addressing). روز فرخ is a noun + adjective 'an auspicious/fortunate day', and کنیم (1st pl. subjunctive/volitional) means 'we will make' — together: 'we will set an auspicious day for meeting you.'
segments.s508-g93: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بیامد is the past 3rd‑person 'came'. ترنجی به دست is a descriptive phrase 'with a roundel (ترنجی) in (his) hand' — ترنجی precedes the locative phrase به دست and functions as the object/thing possessed while arriving.
segments.s508-g94: ز = 'from' (poetic). ایوان سالار چین is a chained genitive: 'the iwan/portico of the commander (سالار) of China (چین)'. نیم‌مست is a predicate/adjectival description 'half‑drunk' applied to the subject; the copula is omitted as typical in poetry.
segments.s508-g95: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic/perhaps poetic past verb meaning here 'lifted/rose forth' (the supplied senses are archaic); سر 'head/top' is used figuratively for emergence. از برج شیر 'from the tower/sign of the Lion' indicates origin — likely an astronomical/zodiac image.
segments.s508-g96: سپهر 'the heavens' is the subject. اندر آورد is a compound verbal phrase 'brought in/caused to come' (آورد = 'brought'); شب را is the definite object with the object marker را ('the night'), and به زیر 'down/beneath' is the adverbial goal — 'brought the night down.'
segments.s508-g57: بداریم is a first-person-plural hortative/present form of داشتن used here as 'we will/shall keep' or 'let us keep.' It governs the object phrase همین تاج و تخت ('this very crown and throne'). بر تو is a prepositional phrase placed before the object and means 'for you' or 'to you' (benefactive). Word order: verb – prepositional beneficiary – demonstrative + nouns.
segments.s508-g58: This clause is a protective wish introduced by the negative present/subjunctive نیاید ('may not come'). گزندت is the noun گزند 'harm' plus the enclitic -ت 'to you' (directed object). به چیزی 'by/through anything' gives the instrument/means ('by any thing'), and ز بخت is a source phrase 'from fate/fortune.' The overall construction means 'may no harm come to you from fate.'
segments.s508-g59: وگر is the conditional 'if.' کند here functions as an impersonal/helping verb 'it happens/it befalls' or an auxiliary that combines with the subjunctive باشی to form a conditional sense. باشی is the present subjunctive/optative second-person singular of بودن 'to be' (here part of the periphrasis expressing 'if you should...'). به پیش آمدن is an infinitival locution 'to come before / to appear in the presence of' where به + پیش marks motion/toward the presence and آمدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to come.'
segments.s508-g60: This is a prepositional phrase specifying origin and direction: ز کشور 'from the country/land' and سوی شاه خویش آمدن 'to come toward (or appear before) one's king.' شاه خویش is a possessive/reflexive construction (خوی + enclitic ش) meaning 'one's own king' (here naturally rendered 'your king' in context). آمدن is the infinitive/gerund conveying the action 'coming.'
segments.s508-g61: ز چیزی که is a relative/partitive opening 'of that which / of something that...' باشد is a third-person subjunctive/present ('there may be' or 'is/exists' in optative nuance). طرایف is the plural noun meaning 'delights/curiosities/trinkets' and به چین is a locative phrase 'in the fold(s)' (به marking location 'in' + چین 'fold'). The clause therefore says 'of things that have trinkets in their folds.'
segments.s508-g62: A simple enumerative phrase introduced by ز 'of/from' that lists valuables: زرینه (an adjective used substantively = 'golden / golden things'), followed by conjunctions linking اسپ 'steed/horse', تیغ 'sword', and نگین 'gem'. The nouns are coordinated and describe the kinds of gifts or riches.
segments.s508-g63: هم از marks addition ('also/from'), followed by a coordination of tangible items: جامه 'garment/robe', پرده 'curtain/veil', and تخت عاج 'throne of ivory' (تخت modified by عاج 'ivory'). The phrase lists more material gifts; عاج is an adjectival modifier after تخت in this poetic order.
segments.s508-g64: Another enumerative phrase introduced by ز 'from' listing fine textiles and jewelry: دیبای پرمایه is 'rich/substantial brocade' (دیبا 'brocade' qualified by پرمایه 'substantial, richly made'), followed by طوق 'necklace/collar' and تاج 'crown.' These nouns are coordinated as further examples of the gifts.
segments.s508-g177: 'ببود' is an archaic past copula meaning 'it was' (verb first for poetic effect). The temporal phrase following it — 'آن شب و بامداد پگاه' — lists times ('that night and the break of dawn/early morning') placed after the copula for emphasis.
segments.s508-g178: 'به آرام' is an adverbial phrase ('in a calm manner'), where 'به' functions adverbially ('-ly/by') and 'آرام' is the adjective 'calm'; 'بنشست' is a past-tense third-person singular verb 'he sat down'; 'بر تخت شاه' is a prepositional phrase 'upon the king's throne' (بر + تخت + شاه). Word order: adverbial → verb → prepositional complement.
segments.s508-g179: 'فرستاده را' uses the object marker 'را' to mark 'the messenger' as the definite direct object; 'چیز بخشید' means 'he bestowed/gave a thing' (here بخشید = to give/bestow); 'و گفت' follows as a simple past 'and he said' introducing reported speech.
segments.s508-g180: 'که' is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. 'با تو' ('with you') is a prepositional phrase modifying the following noun; 'روان' is the subject 'soul/spirit'; 'مسیحست' is a fused form of 'مسیح است' ('is the Messiah' / 'is of the Messiah'); 'جفت' ('pair/companion') functions as a predicate noun meaning 'paired/companion'. The clause therefore says roughly 'that the soul is (as) the Messiah's companion / is paired with the Messiah and is with you.'
segments.s508-g181: 'برو' is the imperative 'go'. 'پیش فغفور' is the prepositional phrase 'into the presence of the Faghfur' (the Byzantine emperor). 'چینی بگوی' is an instruction 'speak Chinese' or 'tell (it) in Chinese' — 'چینی' modifies the verb 'بگوی' (speak/tell). The two imperatives are given in sequence: go and speak.
segments.s508-g182: 'که' introduces the content of what to say ('that'). 'نزدیک ما یافتی' literally 'you obtained/arrived near us' — here 'یافتی' is best read as 'you gained/obtained' (i.e., by approaching us you gained something). 'آب‌روی' ('reputation/honor') is the object: together the clause means 'that by coming near us you have obtained reputation/favor.' Word order: subordinate marker → adverbial 'نزدیک' + object 'ما' → verb 'یافتی' → object/result 'آب‌روی'.
segments.s508-g183: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'ایدر' is the adverb 'here'. 'بباشی' is a present/subjunctive second-person form meaning 'you remain / you are' in the protasis of a conditional. 'همی' is an emphatic particle ('indeed/then'); 'چین تراست' = 'China is yours' where 'تراست' (ت + راست) functions as 'it is yours/it belongs to you' (poetic fused form).
segments.s508-g184: 'وگر' = 'but if / and if' (alternative conditional). 'جای دیگر' = 'another place' (جای + modifier دیگر). 'خرامی' is a noun/gerund meaning 'proud stride / strutting'. 'رواست' is 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا + ست copula). The clause says that if you go elsewhere, strutting proudly there is appropriate/permitted.
segments.s508-g17: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی is a noun 'news/information' (what arrives). آمد is the past tense 'came/arrived'. به marks the recipient (indirect object) 'to', and فغفور is the addressee/title 'the emperor'. ازین is the compound of از + این used to introduce the following subordinate clause meaning 'about/that concerning this'.
segments.s508-g18: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of the news. آمد is the past verb 'had come'. فرستاده‌ای is a participial/nominal form 'a sent one / a messenger' (indefinite noun formed from فرستادن). سوی چین is a prepositional phrase: سوی 'toward' + چین 'China', indicating the direction/destination.
segments.s508-g19: پذیره is the noun 'reception/acceptance' used with فرستاد here in a light-verb construction meaning 'had them received' / 'received (them)'. فرستاد is past 'sent' (the light verb). چندی is an adverb/adjective 'some/several'. سپاه is the noun 'army/force' serving as the thing sent.
segments.s508-g20: سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander'. گرازان is an epithet/noun 'boars' or figuratively 'a troop of boars', here qualifying Alexander's forces. بیامد is past 'came/arrived'. به راه is an adverbial phrase 'on the road' (به marking direction/place + راه 'road').
segments.s508-g21: چو is the temporal 'when'. آمد is the past 'came'. بران is an archaic/contracted form (بر + آن) meaning 'to/at that' (i.e. 'to that place'). بارگاه بزرگ is the noun phrase 'the great palace' (بارگاه 'palace' modified by بزرگ 'great').
segments.s508-g22: بدید is the past simple 'he saw' (main verb of the clause). آن is the demonstrative 'that' anchoring the following noun phrase. گزیده is an adjective/participle 'chosen/elite' modifying سپاه 'army', and بزرگ is another adjective 'great' — adjectives follow the noun in this line and stack after آن.
segments.s508-g23: بیامد is the past 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking origin. دهلیز is 'corridor/vestibule' (place of origin). تا gives the endpoint 'up to/until' and پیش اوی 'in his presence' (پیش 'before/in the presence of' + اوی an older form of the third‑person clitic/pronoun 'him'). The clause expresses motion from a place up to the sovereign's presence.
segments.s508-g24: پراندیشه is an adjectival compound 'full of thought / thoughtful / careworn' describing mental disposition. جان here is a noun 'life/soul' used as the thing concerned. بداندیش is an adjective/noun meaning 'ill‑wisher, malicious (one who thinks ill)'. اوی is the old third‑person clitic/pronoun 'he/him', attached to identify the person. Taken together the phrase is a piled epithet describing a malicious, anxious person standing before the ruler.
segments.s508-g137: به دل گفت is a light verb phrase meaning 'he said to his heart' (i.e., he told himself); به marks the indirect object (to), دل is the noun 'heart', and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. ازین پس is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس), and کس اندر جهان forms the noun phrase 'anyone / no one in the world' (اندر = 'in'). The negative that makes 'کس' mean 'no one' appears on the verb in the next segment.
segments.s508-g138: نبیند is the negative-present/subjunctive form of 'to see' used here functionally as 'will not (shall not) see'; it carries the negation that applies to کس in the previous segment. مرا is the object 'me' written as two clitics (م + را) where را is the definite-object marker attached to the pronoun; رفته is a perfect/participle 'gone' describing the subject (i.e. 'having gone'), and جایی نهان is the adverbial/locative 'to a hidden place' (جایی = somewhere/place, نهان = hidden). Together the clause means 'no one in the world will see me — (I) having gone to a hidden place.'
segments.s508-g139: ز ایوان بیامد is a simple past action 'he came from the iwan' (ز = 'from', ایوان = portico/iwan, بیامد = 'came'). به جای نشست is a successive action 'sat in (his) place' (به marks location, جای = place, نشست = sat). The subject is omitted (third person) and the two verb phrases are sequential actions of the same subject.
segments.s508-g140: میان here functions as a temporal/connecting adverb ('meanwhile' / 'then' / 'in between'), introducing the next action. از پی بازگشتن is a nominalized infinitive phrase 'the following/attempt to return' (از پی = 'after/following', بازگشتن = 'to return'), and ببست is the past tense 'he shut/closed' with the object (the way/door) omitted but understood. The whole means he closed the access so that returning would be prevented.
segments.s508-g141: سرافراز فغفور is a nominative phrase 'the lofty/exalted emperor' (سرافراز = exalted/proud, فغفور = title for the emperor/Caesar). بگشاد is an honorific/poetic past-form meaning 'opened' (verb) and takes گنج ('treasure') as its object; the order is subject – verb – object.
segments.s508-g142: ز بخشش ('from/by his giving' or 'because of his generosity') is an ablative prepositional phrase; نیامد is the negative past 'did not come' used impersonally here, به دلش = 'to his heart' (دل + possessive suffix ش = 'his'), and ایچ رنج = 'any/single pain' where ایچ = 'nothing/none' negates رنج. Together: 'from his generosity no pain came to his heart' (i.e., he felt no sorrow at giving).
segments.s508-g143: نخستین is an adverb/adjective 'first' (here adverbial: 'first of all'), and بفرمود is a courtly/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered' (an honorific use of گفت/فرمود) taking the direct object پنجاه تاج ('fifty crowns' — numeric + noun). The construction is subject (implied) + verb + object.
segments.s508-g144: به گوهر بیاگنده is an adjectival/prepositional phrase modifying 'تاج' — به گوهر = 'with gems' (i.e., gem-studded), and بیاگنده here is a poetic adjective/participle meaning 'adorned' or 'set with' (it modifies تاج). ده تخت عاج is a parallel noun phrase 'ten thrones of ivory' (ده = ten, تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). Word order groups the ornamented crowns first, then the ivory thrones.
segments.s508-g73: بگوی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form built on گفتن with the prefixed بـ used in classical Persian to mean 'say' or 'you should say'. آنچ دانی is a noun phrase 'that which you know' (آنچ = 'that which'; دانی = 'you know'). ز governs the following noun (گفتار اوی) and here functions as a poetic preposition 'about/of/from'. گفتار اوی = 'his speech' (گفتار 'speech' modified by the enclitic/his اوی).
segments.s508-g74: ز at the start takes a series of nouns governed by it: 'about/of'. بالا, مردی, دیدار are three coordinated nouns (joined by و) giving attributes; اوی ('his') attaches to the final noun but by syntax and meaning scopes over the whole list (i.e. 'his loftiness, his manliness, and his appearance').
segments.s508-g75: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (گفتن, 3rd sg. past) and introduces direct speech. ای is the vocative 'O', and سپهدار چین is a vocative title 'commander (sepahdar) of Chin/China'.
segments.s508-g76: کسی چون سکندر مدان بر زمین is an elliptical interrogative: کسی 'anyone', چون 'like/as', followed by the name سکندر (Alexander) possibly with an epithet مدان. بر زمین is a locative 'on/over the earth' (i.e. 'on earth'). Together: 'Is there anyone on earth like Alexander?'
segments.s508-g77: به introduces a list of qualities in which the person is praised: به مردی 'in manliness', و رادی 'and magnanimity' (رادی = generosity/nobility), و بخش 'and bounty/giving' (بخش here = generosity/gift), و خرد 'and wisdom'. The repeated و coordinates the nouns; به governs the whole series as an adverbial frame.
segments.s508-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'beyond/from' and takes اندیشهِ هر کسی 'the thought of every person' as its object. بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن with prefixed بـ used in classical narrative; here it means 'passes/exceeds' (i.e. 'he surpasses what any man's thought can reach').
segments.s508-g79: به بالای سروست is a comparative/figurative phrase: به + بالای + سرو (سروست) = 'he is above/as tall as the cypress' (lofty). و با زور پیل links another attribute with با marking instrument/means: 'and with the strength of an elephant' (با زور = 'with the force/strength of', پیل = 'elephant', a classical epic comparison for great strength).
segments.s508-g80: به بخشش begins another adverbial phrase 'in generosity' (بخشش here = giving/granting). به کردار دریای نیل repeats the prepositional frame 'in manner/behavior like the sea of the Nile' — کردار 'conduct/deed', دریای نیل 'the sea of the Nile' (a poetic simile for vast, overflowing generosity). The two به-phrases specify domains in which he is compared to great natural and animal forces.
segments.s508-g169: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دستور is the noun 'order/command' as the grammatical subject. با لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'with the army' (با + noun). آمدش is past tense آمد plus the enclitic ش (= 'to him'), so 'came to him'. پیش here means 'before/in the presence of'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal clause (چو ...) + subject + circumstantial phrase + verb+clitic + location/adverb.
segments.s508-g170: بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (بـ- prefix + گفت). آنچ is the relative/indefinite compound 'that which'. آمد is past 'came' (here 'had come/arrived'). ز = 'from' (poetic form). بازار خویش is 'his bazaar/market' where خویش is the reflexive/possessive ('his own') split into خوی + ش in the tokenization. Together this clause is the direct object of بگفت: 'he said that which had come from his own bazaar.'
segments.s508-g171: سپاهش is سپاه + ش ('his army'). برو here functions as a particle adverbially placed before the verb to indicate immediacy/sequence ('thereupon/then' — in older poetic usage it sits before the verb). خواندند is the past plural 'they cried/read/uttered'. آفرین is the exclamation/acclamation they uttered. The whole segment reports: 'his army then cried “Afarin.”'
segments.s508-g172: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject; برنهادند is a compound past verb (بر + نهادند) meaning 'they raised' (note نهادن used with بر here = to raise up). سر 'head' is the object and بر زمین 'from/on the ground' indicates the relation: in context the usual reading is that they had been prostrate and then lifted their heads. Word order Subject–Verb–Object is inverted poetically: 'All (subject) lifted (verb) their heads (object) from/on the ground.'
segments.s508-g173: بدانست is a past simple verb meaning 'he knew/perceived'. چینی is the noun 'the Chinese' (here a person). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/that which' and links to او هست شاه 'he is king' — a copular clause with هست. Together: 'He perceived that the Chinese was a king.'
segments.s508-g174: پیاده بیامد is a simple past clause 'he came on foot' (پیاده = 'on foot', بیامد = past 'came'). غریوان is a noun meaning 'neckline/collar, bosom' placed after the verb as a circumstantial/detail (poetic order), and به راه 'on/along the road' is a locative phrase. The segment strings these details: 'he came on foot, (with) his collar/bosom to/along the road.'
segments.s508-g175: سکندر (proper name) is the speaker. بدو is the clitic/proclitic phrase 'to him' (ب + دو = 'to him'). گفت is used as the past reporting verb 'said'. پوزش مکن is an imperative negative: پوزش 'apology' + مکن 'do not (make)' — literally 'do not make apology' = 'do not apologize.'
segments.s508-g176: مران is here either a proper name or an imperative/verb-form depending on context; in this line it functions as an instruction ('bring/lead' or a name addressed). پیش فغفور means 'before the Faghfur' (فغفور = the emperor; پیش = in the presence of). زین در سخن is a short phrase that literally reads 'from/in this (saddle) speech' — syntactically it indicates where or in what manner to speak (زین 'from/at this [poetic 'saddle' or 'place'] + در سخن 'in speech/court'). Overall it issues the command to present someone before the emperor and address him there.
segments.s508-g97: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). به نزدیک + فغفور is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward/near the emperor”; Persian here places the direction phrase before the verb. شد is the past/main verb meaning “became/arrived/approached” (classical usage), so the clause reads literally “Alexander became near to the emperor” = “Alexander approached the emperor.”
segments.s508-g98: از اندیشه بد = “from (out of) bad thought”; دلش is دل + enclitic ش (his heart). دور شد is a compound verbal idea: دور (distant/removed) + شد (became) so the phrase means “was made distant / was removed” → “the bad thought left his heart.” Word order puts the source (از اندیشه بد) before the possessor (دلش) and the resulting verb (دور شد).
segments.s508-g99: بپرسید is the past tense ‘asked’ (third person). ز here is the poetic/prepositional from (از) and the following و is functioning as the clitic pronoun (او) written separately in this apparatus—together they mean “from him” (i.e., “asked him”). گفت is the report verb “he said.” شب چون بدی here begins the reported question: شب = “night/last night,” چون = introducing a question sense “when/how,” and بدی is used as an adjective/noun idea “bad/ill” in the original lexicon (in context it introduces the complaint about a bad action).
segments.s508-g100: که is the subordinating conjunction “that/when.” بیرون شدی = بیرون + شدی, a past form meaning “you went out” or “you went forth” (شدی = you became/you went). دوش is an adverbial temporal “last night.” میگون is a proper name (here a person). بدی is lexicalized here as “evil/bad” — the clause asks whether, when he went out last night, he did harm to Mi-gun. The clause is a reported question introduced by گفت + که.
segments.s508-g101: ازان پس (“thereupon/after that”) gives the temporal link. بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning “he commanded/ordered.” تا here introduces a purposive or result clause (“so that/that”), and شد دبیر literally “a scribe should/might be (made/come)” — i.e., he ordered that a scribe be present or be brought. The sequence (بفرمود + تا + شد) is a classical way to introduce what is to be done.
segments.s508-g102: بیاورد is the third‑person subjunctive/present used after the command (here reported via تا + بفرمود) and means “bring” or “should bring.” قرطاس و مشک و عبیر is a simple list of objects (paper, musk, and fragrance/ambergris) marked by the coordinating و. The verb governs the whole list: “that the scribe bring paper, musk, and ambergris.”
segments.s508-g103: مران is the subject (a proper name). نامه را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker (‘the letter’). زود is an adverb ‘quickly/soon.’ پاسخ نوشت is a two‑word verbal idea meaning “wrote a reply” (نوشت is the past tense ‘wrote’). Word order: Subject + object + adverb + verb (typical Persian poetic order).
segments.s508-g104: بیاراست is a poetic past form of آراستن ‘to adorn,’ meaning ‘he adorned’ or ‘he made beautiful.’ قرطاس را again is the object with the marker را (‘the paper’). چون is the simile/comparative “like/as,” and بهشت is the simile’s standard ‘paradise/garden.’ Together: “he adorned the paper like paradise.”
segments.s508-g25: دوان is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'ran' or 'rushed' and here sets the actional manner; پیش او رفت is a normal past construction 'went/advanced before him' (پیش = 'in the presence of'); و links the clauses. بردش uses برد with enclitic -ش ('him/it') meaning 'took/led (him)' — in context 'took him to (perform) نماز' — and نماز is the definite noun 'prayer' (here the activity/place of prayer). Word order is compact/poetic: manner + motion (دوان ... رفت) then coordinated verb carrying an object (برد + -ش) then the object noun.
segments.s508-g26: نشست is the simple past 'sat' (third person) describing the ensuing posture; اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and governs ایوان 'iwan/porch'; زمانی دراز is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' (زمانی = 'a while/for a time', دراز = 'long') placed after the place to indicate duration.
segments.s508-g27: بپرسید is the past narrative 'asked' (here the subject is فغفور immediately following: 'فغفور بپرسید' = 'the Caesar asked'). و connects the next action. بنواختش is a past-tense causative/active 'had it struck/played' with the enclitic -ش = 'it/him' (i.e. some instrument or tune was made to sound for/at him). This is a compact classical word order where subject, verb and then a coordinated verb with its object are placed in sequence.
segments.s508-g28: یکی نامور جایگه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/famed seat/place' (یکی = 'a certain', نامور = 'renowned', جایگه = 'place/seat'). ساختش is past 'made/built it' with enclitic -ش referring back to that place or to 'him' depending on context; here it means 'he had/made for him a famed seat.' The verb ساخت is transitive and takes the previously introduced noun as its object.
segments.s508-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; برزد is an archaic past-form meaning 'rose/appeared' (here 'came up' or 'lifted'); سر از کوه literally 'head from the mountain' is an idiomatic poetic expression 'lifted its head from the mountain' = 'appeared above the mountain's top'; روشن چراغ 'the bright lamp' is a noun phrase with adjective before noun (روشن = 'bright', چراغ = 'lamp'). Note the poetic inversion: verb (برزد) comes before the full noun phrase it applies to.
segments.s508-g30: ببردند is the past plural 'they carried/took' (action performed by attendants); بالای is a locative 'upon/atop' and takes the following noun as location; زرین 'golden' modifies جناغ ('breast/chest') — poetically, 'the golden breast' denotes a gilded front or breastplate-like ornament of the hall. The direct object of بَرَدَند (the lamp) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s508-g31: فرستاده here 'envoy/messenger' is the noun; شاه را uses شاه with the object marker را to indicate the definite relation 'the king's' or to mark the definite object as a unit (i.e. 'the king's envoy'); پیش خواند is a compound verbal phrase 'called before / summoned' (پیش = 'before/in presence of', خواند here = 'called/summoned'). The overall construction means 'he summoned the king's envoy.'
segments.s508-g32: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'; فراوان modifies the verb as an adverb 'much/abundantly' (i.e. 'many'); سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches'. براند is a past form of راند used here in a poetic sense 'uttered/put forth' (classically glossed as 'drove/put forth' but in context means 'he spoke/uttered'). The line thus reports that Alexander spoke many words.
segments.s508-g65: ز چیزی که یابی فرستی به گنج is a compact clause meaning “from/whatever thing that you find, send (it) to the treasury.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/whatever,” followed by چیزی (indefinite). که introduces a relative clause whose verb یابی is 2nd‑person present (here functioning as the relative ‘that you find’). فرستی is a 2nd‑person jussive/imperative-like form ‘send’ and به گنج marks the goal with به + noun.
segments.s508-g66: چو خواهی که از ما نیایدت رنج is a conditional/desiderative clause: چو here = “if/when (that)”; خواهی is 2nd‑person present 'you wish/want' used to introduce the desired outcome; که introduces the subordinate clause. از ما is the source 'from us'; نیایدت is negative optative/subjunctive (نیاید) with the enclitic ت 'to you' meaning 'may not come to you'; رنج is the thing feared (hardship).
segments.s508-g67: سپاه مرا بازگردان ز راه is an imperative clause: سباه (سپاه) 'army' with the possessive/object marking مرا ('my' / 'the army of me' with را as the definite object marker). بازگردان is a 2nd‑person imperative/jussive 'return/turn back'. ز راه is a source phrase 'from the road/route' (ز = from + راه = road). Note Persian allows the object phrase before the verb.
segments.s508-g68: بباش ایمن از گنج و تخت و کلاه is an imperative exhortation: بــباش (be) is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of بودن; ایمن is a predicative adjective 'safe/protected (from)'; از + noun lists the things to be guarded against — here گنج, تخت, کلاه (treasure, throne, crown). The coordinating و links the nouns in a simple 'safe from X, Y, and Z' construction.
segments.s508-g69: چو سالار چین زان نشان نامه دید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'; سالار چین is a compound noun 'the commander (سالار) of China (چین)' where چین is the place-name; زان is a demonstrative 'that' (literally 'from that/that'); نشان نامه is a compound 'token/insignia-letter' (a letter bearing a sign); دید is the simple past 'saw/beheld.'
segments.s508-g70: برآشفت و پس خامشی برگزید contains two coordinated past actions: برآشفت is an intransitive past 'he was angered/he grew angry'; و پس links to the next clause ('and then'); خامشی برگزید is a transitive-like structure 'he chose silence' (خامشی = 'silence' as object, برگزید = past 'chose/selected'), idiomatically 'held his peace.'
segments.s508-g71: بخندید و پس با فرستاده گفت is narrative past: بخندید = 'he laughed' (past 3rd person); و پس links the sequence ('and then'); با فرستاده is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the messenger' (با marking accompaniment/with); گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' introducing indirect speech.
segments.s508-g72: که شاه ترا آسمان باد جفت is the reported utterance. که introduces indirect speech 'that' (or is the quotative that); شاه is the noun 'king' as the topic/subject of the wish; ترا is the 2nd‑person enclitic 'to you' (here split as ت + را in the word list); آسمان باد is an optative construction where باد is the optative particle 'may it be' with آسمان 'heaven' supplying the agency/instrument; جفت is the predicate noun 'a match/mate.' Together: 'May heaven make the king your match.'
segments.s508-g153: بیاورد is a narrative past (3rd person) meaning “he brought” and appears before its objects (poetic word order). زین is the noun “saddle/trappings” used here partitive/locative (‘from each saddle’), هر یکی = “each/one of them” and ده هزار is the numeral phrase that quantifies what was brought.
segments.s508-g154: This clause completes the verbal idea: خردمند functions as a collective/epic adjective/noun ‘wise (people)’; گنجور is an adjectival epithet ‘treasure‑laden/treasury‑full’ modifying بار; بربست is past (3rd sing.) ‘he bound/packed’ and بار is the noun ‘load/cargo.’ Together: he packed a treasure‑filled load of wise ones.
segments.s508-g155: گرانمایه صد زین is a noun phrase ‘a hundred precious saddles’ (adjective before noun, numeral after). به marks accompaniment/quality (‘with’), سیمین is an adjective ‘silver’ and ستام is a poetic noun/epithet (here functioning as ‘adorned/praised’), describing the silver trappings.
segments.s508-g156: ز introduces the next specification (‘of/from’ or ‘and/or’ in poetic enumeration) and زرینه ‘golden’ modifies an implied noun (here continuing ‘saddles’), پنجاه gives the number fifty (understood to apply to that noun). بردند is past (3rd pl.) ‘they carried/took,’ and نام normally means ‘name’ or ‘fame’ — here it appears at the end of the line as a concise poetic object/closing (often rendered simply in English as ‘ones’ or left implicit when listing goods).
segments.s508-g157: ببردند is past (3rd pl.) ‘they carried/took.’ سیصد شتر is the quantified noun phrase ‘three hundred camels.’ سرخ‌موی is a post‑nominal compound adjective ‘red‑haired’ modifying شتر (typical Persian adjective‑after‑noun pattern or compound formation).
segments.s508-g158: طرایف here means ‘curios/oddities’ (things of wonder). بدو + دار functions as ‘in cases/containers for them’ (بدو = ‘to/for it/him/them’, دار = ‘holder/box’ used as container). چینی is adjectival ‘Chinese/porcelain’ and بدوی ‘ancient/original’ qualifies the make; together they describe the curios as being in Chinese, antique cases.
segments.s508-g159: یکی مرد is the indefinite ‘a certain man’ (یکی used as ‘a certain/someone’). با introduces instruments/attributes (‘with’); سنگ ‘stone’ is literal; و links the coordinate attribute; شیرین modifies سخن forming شیرین سخن ‘sweet‑spoken’ (an adjectival phrase meaning ‘sweet of speech’ or ‘eloquent’).
segments.s508-g160: گزین کرد is a compound/prefinal verb: گزین (choose) + کرد (do) yielding past ‘he chose.’ زان is a demonstrative/prepositional particle ‘from that/from those,’ and چینیان کهن = ‘the ancient Chinese’ (چینیان = Chinese people/things; کهن = old/ancient). The whole clause: ‘he chose (one) from those ancient Chinese.’
segments.s508-g105: نخست (adverb) 'first' comes at the clause start to set sequence. آفرین کرد is a compound past: آفرین (here the verbal root 'to create') + کرد (past tense auxiliary 'did/made'), an archaic/poetic way to say 'created'. بر marks the beneficiary/target ('for/on/upon'), so بر دادگر means 'for the just one'.
segments.s508-g106: خداوند is the subject (here 'the Lord/God'), placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian. مردی، داد و هنر are a coordinated list of direct objects ('a man, justice, and skill/virtue'), joined by و. داد is the noun 'justice' not a verb here.
segments.s508-g107: This segment lists the qualities hoped for: خداوند ('God' or 'the Lord') followed by three nouns joined by و (فرهنگ و پرهیز و دین) — 'culture, abstinence/forbearance and religion'. The order is noun listing; no finite verb appears here because the optative follows.
segments.s508-g108: ازو باد is an optative construction: ازو 'from him' (i.e., from God) + باد (optative/copula meaning 'may it be'). بر شاه روم آفرین literally 'upon the King of Rome (be) āfarīn' — here آفرین functions as an exclamatory/praiseful word ('praise/blessing'). بر is the preposition 'upon/on'.
segments.s508-g109: رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached' and precedes its noun phrase (typical classical order). این فرستاده is the subject 'this messenger', and چرب‌گوی is a postpositive adjective 'smooth‑tongued'/'flattering' modifying فرستاده.
segments.s508-g110: هم can mean 'also' or emphasize 'that very'; here it links to the preceding arrival and introduces the object borne: آن نامه 'that letter'. شاه فرهنگ‌جوی is a compound 'the culture‑seeking king' (فرهنگ + جوی 'seeker/searcher'), so the whole phrase means 'that letter of the culture‑seeking king' (understood as 'arrived bearing that letter').
segments.s508-g111: سخنهای شاهان is a genitive construction 'the words/sayings of the kings' (سخنهای = plural/construct form). همه quantifies ('all/everyone') and خواندم is first‑person past 'I read/recited'. The verb at the end is standard Persian word order for a main clause.
segments.s508-g112: وزان (وز آن) is a conjunctive phrase 'and/from that (thus, because of that)'. با بزرگان 'with the great ones' (the nobles/elders) is a prepositional phrase; سخن راندم is an idiomatic expression meaning 'I exchanged words / I conversed' (سخن = speech, راندم here functions as the verb 'to drive/propel' used idiomatically for speaking).
segments.s508-g161: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' and here introduces a purpose clause. تا introduces that purpose ('so that / in order that'); the phrase با درود و خرام is an adverbial accompaniment ('with greeting and with a proud gait'), with درود 'greeting' and خرام a nominalized gait describing how one should approach.
segments.s508-g162: بیاید is a subjunctive/jussive present ('may/should come') used after تا to express the commanded purpose. بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/ before the king' (بر + noun). The coordination و آرد پیام links two actions of the (understood) subject: 'come before the king and bring a message' — آرد is a present/optative of آوردن 'bring' and پیام is the object 'message'.
segments.s508-g163: که here introduces a subordinate clause giving the intended circumstance ('so that / such that'). یک چند is an adverbial phrase 'for a short time / for a little while' (چند = 'some, a few'). باشد is an optative/subjunctive of بودن meaning 'may be / remain'. به نزدیک چین is a prepositional phrase 'near China' (به + نزدیک + چین) expressing location.
segments.s508-g164: برو is an imperative or hortative 'go' (here functioning as an instruction or transition). نامداران is the plural substantive 'the renowned/illustrious ones' and کند(ند) is the present-subjunctive/optative plural 'let them do / may they do' — here 'کنند آفرین' = 'let them make acclamation' (آفرین = 'acclamation/bravo'). The clause is a terse imperative-cum-wish common in epic style.
segments.s508-g165: فرستاده شد is a passive construction: فرستاده (past participle 'sent') plus شد as auxiliary gives 'was sent'. با سکندر is an accompaniment phrase 'with Alexander'. به راه is a locative/directional phrase 'on the way / on the road', formed with به + راه.
segments.s508-g166: گمانی is a noun 'a supposition / rumour' and is followed by clauses introduced by که; the first که introduces the content of that supposition. The internal verbs are archaic/elliptical: بردی and اویست are compressed/classical forms within nested clauses (here conveying 'you took/thought' and the fused form 'او است' = 'he is'), so the whole fragment means 'the supposition that you took him to be the king.' Classical Persian often uses stacked که-clauses for indirect reports.
segments.s508-g167: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ملاح is the subject 'the helmsman/sailor'. روی سکندر is the direct object 'the face/countenance of Alexander' (noun + genitive), and بدید is the simple past 'he saw'. The clause follows the SOV order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s508-g168: سبک زورقی is an adjectival phrase 'a light skiff' (سبک 'light' qualifying زورقی 'little boat' formed with the adjectival/relational suffix -ی). بادبان برکشید is a verb phrase 'he raised/hoisted the sail' — بادبان (object) preceding the past verb برکشید.
segments.s508-g145: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of / from', introducing the material or contents; سیمین و زرینه form a coordinate adjectival/material phrase 'silvery and golden' that modifies what follows. اشتر هزار is a classical word-order inversion: noun (اشتر, 'camel') followed by the numeral هزار ('a thousand'), i.e. 'a thousand camels.'
segments.s508-g146: بفرمود is the honorific/past form 'he commanded/ordered.' تا here has a purposive sense 'so that / in order that' linking the command to its intended result. برنهادند is a past plural form (بر‑ + نهادند from نهادن) meaning 'they set up / lifted / laden (the saddles/loads)'; بار is the object 'load, cargo.' The clause thus expresses that he ordered the camels to be loaded.
segments.s508-g147: ز ('of') begins a list of materials. دیبای چینی is a two-word phrase 'Chinese brocade' (noun + postposed adjective in classical style). The conjunction و links خز ('fur/velvet') and حریر ('silk') as further items in the list.
segments.s508-g148: Again ز introduces another group: کافور ('camphor') is the named material. وز here is a compact poetic sequence (equivalent to و ز 'and of') joining the next item; مشک ('musk'), بوی ('scent') and عبیر ('fragrance') follow joined by و. The whole segment continues the enumeration of scented and exotic goods.
segments.s508-g149: هزار اشتر is again the classical ordering 'a thousand camels' (noun then numeral). بارکش is a descriptive compound 'load‑bearing / pack‑camel' modifying اشتر. بار کرد is a simple past clause 'carried the load' (کرد as the light past verb).
segments.s508-g150: تن‌آسان is an adjective meaning 'soft‑bodied / luxurious', شد is the past 'became.' آنکو is the archaic relative 'he who / that one who' (contraction of آن که). درم خوار is a noun phrase 'one who scorns/despises the dirham' (درم = dirham; خوار = contemptible/despiser), and کرد is the light past verb used with the compound. Together the clause says 'he who treated the dirham as worthless became pampered.'
segments.s508-g151: ز introduces another list of materials: سنجاب ('squirrel', here meaning squirrel fur), و links قاقم (an obscure item/name) as a co‑item, and the next ز repeats 'of' before موی سمور ('the hair/fur of the sable'), specifying another material. The repeated ز is a normal classical device to mark each sub‑item.
segments.s508-g152: ز again 'of', گستردنیها is a plural substantive formed from an adjective/verb meaning 'things to be spread' (i.e. coverlets, spreads). و links جام بلور, a noun phrase 'a cup/goblet (جام) of crystal (بلور)'. The phrase completes the catalogue of precious and domestic items.
segments.s508-g121: چو here is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” فرجامشان is the noun فرجام (“end, outcome”) with the 3rd-person plural possessive ـشان, “their end.” روز رزم تو is a genitive chain (روزِ رزمِ تو) meaning “the day of your battle,” and بود is the past copula placed at the end: “their end was the day of your battle.”
segments.s508-g122: زمانه is a personified “time/fortune.” The line uses a paired negation: نه ... نخواهد ... to express “neither ... nor ...”; کاهد is the stem meaning “diminish/lessen,” and نخواهد is the future auxiliary “will (not),” governing فزود “increase.” Together: “the times/fortune will neither decrease nor increase.”
segments.s508-g123: تو is the explicit subject “you.” زیشان (ز + ایشان) means “from/of them” and functions as the object. مکن is the negative imperative “do not.” کشی here is a verbal noun meaning “slaying/killing” (classical use), so مکن کشی = “do not slay.” و برتری links with “and [do not seek] superiority,” the second member relying on the same negative. Word order (object before verb) is normal in Persian.
segments.s508-g124: که‌گر is the conditional “if.” ز آهنی literally “from (or of) iron,” here giving the sense “as if of iron / beyond iron.” بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly.” بگذری is a present/subjunctive/potential form of گذریدن “to pass/exceed,” so the clause states the hypothetical: “if you pass by (as) iron, without doubt ...”.
segments.s508-g125: کجا شد is a rhetorical question “where/what became of …?” with شد used in the sense “what happened to / where are.” The three proper names are simply coordinated objects of the question: “What became of Fereydun, Zahhak, and Jam?”
segments.s508-g126: فراز is an adverb “on high / aloft.” آمد is the past of آمدن “came/rose.” از باد indicates source/means “by/from the wind.” و شد سوی uses شد as “turned/became” plus سوی “toward,” and دم here means “breath” (metonymically the moment of death or the vital breath). The clause pictures them borne aloft by the wind and heading toward the ‘breath’/end.
segments.s508-g127: من is the subject “I.” از تو with نترسم gives the phrase “I do not fear you” (نترسم = present negative of ترسیدن). The second nominal clause began with نه to continue the negation: نه جنگ آورم = “nor will I bring/cause war,” where آورم is the present/future-like form of آوردن (“bring/cause”).
segments.s508-g128: نه begins the second negative (“nor”). بر سان تو is a classical order meaning “in the manner of you / like you” (بر + سان + pronoun). باد here functions as an optative particle (“may/let [it be that] a wind …”) rather than simply ‘wind,’ and گیرد is the verb “seize/take.” سرم is the object “my head.” Together the clause expresses a wish/contrast: “nor may a wind seize my head as it did yours.”
segments.s508-g113: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a source or inventory. دارای داراب و فریان و فور is a noun phrase listing possessions or attributes (داراب = the name Darab; فریان = plural/collective of فر, 'royal glory'; فور here is a poetic noun 'abundance/surge'). Word order places ز before the whole list for a condensed, poetic catalogue.
segments.s508-g114: This is a condensed relative/clausal string: سخن هرچ پیدا بد از رزم و سور = 'whatever speech/tale is found about battle and feast.' هرچ = 'whatever'; پیدا = 'found/discovered'; بد here is an archaic/predicative form (see note) functioning like بود 'was' (i.e. 'was found'). از introduces the domains 'رزم و سور' (battle and banquet/feast).
segments.s508-g115: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'that/for'. پیروز گشتی is a poetic past: پیروز = 'victorious', گشتی here functions with it to mean 'you became (victorious)' (an idiomatic verbal phrase). بریشان = بر + ایشان 'over/against them'. همه modifies the following idea 'all of them.' Note the compact, elevated diction typical of Shahnameh.
segments.s508-g116: شبان بودی = 'you were a shepherd' (شبان = shepherd; بودی past of بودن 'were'). The clause is nominative/predicative. و links the next verbless nominal clause: شهریاران رمه = 'the rulers/kings (شهریاران) [were] a flock (رمه)', where the verb 'were' is understood from بودی and omitted for poetic economy.
segments.s508-g117: This line features poetic inversion: داد (gave) appears before its subject and object for effect. The sense is: 'خداوند خورشید و ماه دادَت' — 'the Lord of sun and moon gave you...'. تو is the recipient 'you'; داد is the past 'gave'; خداوند خورشید و ماه is the lofty subject 'the Lord/master of Sun and Moon.'
segments.s508-g118: به introduces the beneficiary/recipient of what was given: به مردی مدان = 'to/for a man named Madan' (مدان is here treated as a proper name); و فزون سپاه = 'and an abundant/large army' (فزون = abundant; سپاه = army). The sequence lists what God bestowed.
segments.s508-g119: چو = 'when'. بر مهتری بگذرد روزگار is an impersonal/time clause: بر مهتری 'upon preeminence' (مهتری = preeminence/chiefness) and بگذرد روزگار = 'fate/time passes' (بگذرد is a present/subjunctive form used poetically for general truth: 'passes'). Together: 'When fate passes over greatness/a foremost one.'
segments.s508-g120: This is the correlative clause 'چه ... چه ...' = 'whether ... or ...'. در سور میرد چه در کارزار = 'whether he dies at a feast (سور) or in battle (کارزار)'. میرد is the poetic present/optative form 'dies' used to state a general possibility. The verb is repeated in sense across the alternatives, omitted for economy.
segments.s508-g41: دگر here is an adverb meaning “again/then” (marks a further remark). گفت is the simple past “said.” فرمانِ ما سویِ چین is a noun phrase: فرمان (command/order) possessed by ما (our), modified by سویِ چین (“toward China”); the whole phrase is what is being said (direct speech introduced without quotation marks). Word order is adverb → verb → noun phrase.
segments.s508-g42: چنانست is the fused form of چنان است “it is such/so” and introduces a result or characterization; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows (“such that…”). آباد ماند زمین is a poetic word order: آباد (prosperous/inhabited) + ماند (remained / endured) + زمین (the earth/land) — literally “prosperous remained the land,” i.e. “the land remains prosperous.”
segments.s508-g43: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning “ought not / should not.” بسیچید is the verb “to muster / to gather (for war)” presented after the modal (here conveying prohibition: ‘ought not to muster’). ما را is the object “us” with the accusative marker را, and به جنگ is the prepositional phrase “into/for war.” The clause expresses a prohibition against being gathered for battle.
segments.s508-g44: که begins a causal/explanatory clause here (“for/because”). از جنگ “from/ because of war” gives the cause. شد (past of شدن) means “became” or “came to be.” روز is the subject “day/time,” and بر فور تنگ is an adverbial phrase: فور “sudden/suddenly” modifying تنگ “narrow/constrained,” so the clause says roughly “because of war the day (times) became suddenly constrained” (i.e. times grew hard/pressed).
segments.s508-g45: چو is a simile marker “as/like.” دارا here is the proper name (Dāra/Darius) used with a following relative clause. که introduces that relative clause “who…”; بد شهریار جهان is a description: بد (bad/ill) modifies شهریار (king/ruler) and جهان (of the world) specifies his dominion — “who was an ill/bad king of the world.”
segments.s508-g46: The second چو continues the comparison (“as/like”). فریان is a poetic plural/collective of فر meaning “royal glory/splendours.” تازی specifies whose splendour (“of the Tazi/Arabs”), and و دیگر مهان links “and other guests/visitors/nobles.” The phrase reads as a parallel simile: ‘as Dāra …, as the splendours of the Arabs and other guests.’
segments.s508-g47: ز (poetic for از) is the preposition “from.” خاور = “east,” and برو is the imperative “go.” تا marks the endpoint “until/as far as,” در here = “gate,” and باختر = “west/western.” Together: an imperative direction “Go from the east as far as the western gate.”
segments.s508-g48: ز فرمان ما “by/from our command” (ز here indicates source/authority: ‘by our decree’). کس نجوید is a jussive/negative allowance construction meaning “let no one seek” (نجوید is present/subjunctive jussive ‘seek’). گذر (passage) is the object: ‘let no one seek passage contrary to our command’ (i.e. nobody may pass without our order).
segments.s508-g129: The clause begins with the conjunction/causal particle که (here meaning "for/because"). خون ریختن is a verbal noun (gerund) 'shedding of blood' serving as the subject; نیست (third person singular present negative) negates the copula; آیینِ ما (literally 'our custom/practice') is the predicate. Word order is typical Persian: [verbal-noun] + نیست + predicate, meaning 'to shed blood is not our custom.'
segments.s508-g130: نه begins a second negative clause 'nor'. بد کردن is a verbal-noun + light verb construction meaning 'doing evil' (کردن = 'to do'); اندرخور دینِ ما is a predicate phrase: اندرخور 'befitting/suitable to' + دینِ ما 'our religion/faith'. The copula 'is' is omitted (elliptical) and understood, so the clause reads 'nor is doing evil befitting our religion.'
segments.s508-g131: بخوانی is the second-person singular present/subjunctive form of خواندن used here conditionally ('if you call/summon'). مرا is the object 'me' (written here split as م + را in the token list); را is the object marker. بر تو باشد شکست places 'شکست' ('defeat') with the subjunctive/optative verb باشد ('there will be/it be') and the prepositional phrase بر تو ('upon you'), so literal order is 'If you call me, upon you there will be defeat.'
segments.s508-g132: که introduces the reason/explanation ('for/because'). یزدان‌پرستم is a self‑predicate with the first‑person singular suffix -م implied from the speaker ('I worship God' / 'I am a God‑worshipper'); نه negates the alternative, followed by خسروپرست 'a worshipper of the king' (i.e. a sycophant). The copula is omitted; the clause contrasts the speaker's loyalty.
segments.s508-g133: فزون زان is a poetic phrase meaning 'more than that' / 'beyond that' (فزون = more, زان = 'than/from that'); فرستم is the first‑person singular present/subjunctive or simple present used to mean 'I will send' or 'I send'. که introduces a relative clause describing the person: دارایِ منش 'having character' (دارای = possessing, منش = character).
segments.s508-g134: ز بخشش is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from generosity' (ز = 'from/because of', بخشش = 'generosity/donation'). نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative 'there be not / there will not be'. مرا را سرزنش (here written م + را + سرزنش) means 'reproach against me' (مرا = me, را = object marker, سرزنش = reproach). The clause states the intended result: 'that, because of generosity, there will be no reproach of me.'
segments.s508-g135: سکندر is the subject. به رخ is a locative/prepositional phrase 'on the face' (به + رخ). رنگِ تشویر is a noun phrase 'the color/complexion of تشویر' (تشویر here is a poetic noun); خورد is the simple past of خوردن used intransitively in the sense 'befell/took (on)' — so the structure is 'Alexander, on the face, a color of ... befell' i.e. 'Alexander's face took on the color of ...'. Note the verb خورد appears at the end, as is common in Persian.
segments.s508-g136: ز گفتار او is an ablative/prepositional phrase 'from his speech/words' (ز = 'from', گفتار = 'speech', او = 'his'). بر جگر تیر خورد places the prepositional phrase بر جگر ('upon the heart/liver' — جگر as the seat of feeling) before the verb خورد 'befell/struck', so literally 'from his words, upon the heart an arrow fell' i.e. 'his words pierced the heart.'
segments.s508-g1: وزان is a poetic compound (و + زان) functioning as a linking adverb meaning 'thereupon' or 'from that'; روی here means 'face/direction' (not physical face) and forms the idiom 'روی ... کشیدن' = 'to turn one's face toward'. لشکر is the subject 'the army' and سوی + noun marks the goal 'toward China'. کشید is a simple past (3rd sg.) of کشیدن 'to draw/turn' governing the whole clause.
segments.s508-g2: سر نامداران is a noun phrase in which سر 'head' (literal/metonymic 'leader') is qualified by نامداران 'the renowned ones'; به بیرون is a directional phrase 'out' or 'to the outside'. کشید here is again past 'drew/pulled' with an implied agent from the context; the whole clause means 'he pulled the chiefs of the famed out'.
segments.s508-g3: همی is a classical/perfective particle giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept on'/'continually'); راند is past of راندن 'drove/marched'. منزل به منزل is a reduplicative adverbial 'from stage to stage' (i.e. from camp to camp). به دشت marks the destination 'into the plain'.
segments.s508-g4: چهل روز is a temporal duration placed before the verb (typical in Persian), تا marks the limit/destination 'as far as' or 'until', and پیش دریا means 'before the sea' / 'in front of the sea'. گذشت is the past 'passed' indicating that that amount of time elapsed until they reached that point.
segments.s508-g5: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'of/from' used here to indicate material or relation ('of brocade'); دیبا is 'brocade' and سراپرده‌ای is an indefinite construction 'an inner curtain/tent'. برکشید is past 'lifted up/drew up' (3rd sg.), so the clause reads 'he lifted up an inner brocade curtain.'
segments.s508-g6: سپه را uses را as the definite object marker ('the army' as direct object). به منزل is the goal 'to a lodging/encampment' and فرود آورید is a compound verb ('فرود آورد' past) meaning 'caused to alight/settled' or here 'brought down/ensconced' (3rd sg. past). Word order (object with را before the verb) is standard.
segments.s508-g7: یکی نامه is 'one/a letter' (یکی = one/a certain), and فرمود here is a polyvalent past meaning 'he uttered/ordered/composed' depending on context (here: 'he composed/ordered'). پس is a connective 'then', and تا begins a purpose or subordinate clause ('so that/in order that') which will take a subjunctive.
segments.s508-g8: نویسد is the 3rd person present/subjunctive form of نوشتن used here in a subordinate purpose clause introduced by تا ('that the scribe write'). ز is the poetic 'about/from' linking the content of the letter, and اسکندر شهرگیر is the noun phrase 'Alexander the besieger' (شهرگیر = besieger/conqueror).
segments.s508-g49: شمار سپاهم is a noun phrase 'the number (شمار) of my host' — the possessive -م attaches to سپاه to give 'my army/host'. نداند is the third‑person singular present/indicative (negative) of دانستن 'to know' and here means 'does not know'. سپهر (the subject) is placed after the verb in this poetic word order: 'the heavens do not know the number of my host.'
segments.s508-g50: وگر introduces a conditional 'but/if', and بشمرد is a potential/subjunctive third‑person singular of شماردن 'to count' meaning 'were to count'. نیز here means 'also/too' and links the following items as included in that counting. ناهید و مهر are proper names of celestial figures (Venus/Anahita and Mithra) — the clause says they too would be counted.
segments.s508-g51: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. هیچ here means 'at all / ever' (i.e. 'if you at any time'). فرمان ما is 'our command/order' with the possessor ما following the noun (typical Persian order). بشکنی is second‑person singular subjunctive/conditional of شکستن 'to break' used here to mean 'you disobey / you break (the command)'.
segments.s508-g52: A three‑item list joined by و: تن 'body/person', بوم 'home/native soil', کشور 'country/realm'. به is the preposition 'to/into/with' and رنج is 'suffering'. افگنی is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form (from a causative element) meaning 'you will cause/bring' or 'causing' — together meaning 'you will inflict suffering'.
segments.s508-g53: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه بخوانی uses the second‑person present/subjunctive بخوانی 'you read' in the temporal clause ('when you read the letter'). بیارای here functions adverbially/adjectivally as 'unadorned/plainly' (read it without embellishment), and ساو is a proper name (Sav) to whom the letter is to be shown; the order is compressed for poetic effect.
segments.s508-g54: مرنجان is an imperative/negative instruction 'do not vex / do not distress' (used here as 'do not distress / do not trouble'). تن خویش = 'your own body' (خویش is split into خوی + ش, with ش the possessive/reflexive clitic '‑his/her/your (self)'); و با بد مکاو joins a second injunction: با 'with', بد 'bad/ill', and مکاو is an archaic/poetic verb/imperative-form whose sense here yields 'do not act badly / do not treat (him) ill'. The whole segment gives two brief prohibitions addressed to the addressee.
segments.s508-g55: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. آیی is second‑person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن 'you come'. بینی is second‑person singular 'you see' and takes مرا (here split into م + را) as its object: 'you will see me'; با سپاه is an adverbial phrase 'with an army'. Poetic word order places the result clause elements close together.
segments.s508-g56: ببینم is first‑person singular (optative/jussive) of دیدن 'I will see/find', used here as the speaker's response to the condition. ت (the consonant shown) plus را together mark 'you' as definite object (t + ra = ترا 'you'). یک‌دل 'single‑hearted' and نیک‌خواه 'benevolent / well‑wisher' are predicative adjectives describing the addressee as the speaker will regard them. The clause therefore states the speaker's assessment on the condition.
segments.s508-g33: بگفت (past tense) = 'he said'; آنچ = relative/indefinite 'that which' or 'what'; بایست here has the sense 'was necessary' (classical/poetic use of بایستن in past sense); و links the two verbs; نامه = direct object 'the letter'; بداد as written looks like a noun in the supplied senses but in the line functions as a past verb 'gave (the letter)'. The clause literally: 'He said that which was necessary and gave the letter.'
segments.s508-g34: سخنهای قیصر = genitive phrase 'the words/speeches of Caesar' (سخنهای = plural 'words'); همه = 'all'; کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد (did/made) combines with the noun یاد ('remembrance') to mean 'recalled' or 'made (a) remembrance of'. Word order is poetic but typical Persian: [object] همه کرد یاد = 'recalled all'.
segments.s508-g35: بران = 'upon that' / 'on it', a locative/discourse pointer; نامه = 'the letter' (object); عنوان = 'heading' or 'title' (what is being set on the letter); بد in the supplied senses would mean 'bad', but here it is not used in that meaning (the correct verbal/orthographic reading differs), so the best reading is that a verb meaning 'set/inscribed' is implied or contracted; از شاه روم = 'from the Shah (King) of Rome' identifying the origin. Classical Persian often omits an explicit verb like 'نهاد' ('placed') so the clause reads as a verbless inscriptional formula.
segments.s508-g36: A string of appositional titles describing that Shah: جهاندار = 'world‑ruler/sovereign'; و = 'and'; سالار = 'lord/chief'; هر مرز و بوم = idiomatic pair 'every border and land' = 'every realm'. These epithets are in apposition to 'شاه روم' and form the formal titulary of the sender.
segments.s508-g37: که = relative 'which/that'; خوانند is 3rd plural present (classical) 'they call/utter/sing' (here 'they call out'); شاهان = 'kings' (subject); برو آفرین is a quoted exclamation/title (آفرین = 'bravo' / acclamation). The clause means 'which kings call out
segments.s508-g38: زما بندگان = 'my/our servants/subjects' (زما is an older possessive form; بندگان here = 'subjects' or 'servants' used honorifically); جهان‑آفرین = epithet 'Creator of the World' (addressed here). In the letter-formula these nouns form a vocative/appositive phrase: '"My servants, O Creator of the World"' as part of the formal salutation. Word order places the possessive before بندگان as usual in older Persian.
segments.s508-g39: A triad of vocative/titular epithets: جهاندار = 'world‑ruler/sovereign'; و = 'and'; داننده = 'knower' (i.e. omniscient); و = 'and'; رهنمای = 'guide' or 'leader'. These are epithets applied (here) to the divine addressee; conjunctions simply link the attributes in a formal enumeration.
segments.s508-g40: خداوند = 'God' or 'the Lord' (vocative/subject of the petition); پاکی و نیکی = 'purity and goodness' (two abstract nouns joined by و); فزای is an optative/imperative jussive form meaning 'increase' / 'may (you/He) increase' — a supplicatory request that God increase purity and goodness. The clause functions as a prayer: 'O Lord, increase purity and goodness.'

sentences.s508-s93: May I rest here, for it is not fitting to hastily march several armies on the road.
sentences.s508-s94: The messenger returned and came like the wind; he delivered Caesar’s message to the faghfur (emperor).
sentences.s508-s5: They wrote every sort — good and bad — as the scribe wrote the letter.
sentences.s508-s6: When Alexander came to power, he chose a messenger — a noble/free‑born one.
sentences.s508-s7: so that with him (the messenger) he might be of one heart and one voice and tell the chief, 'Do or do not.'
sentences.s508-s8: He entrusted the army to the commander of the host, and from those Romans he took five wise men.
sentences.s508-s41: His tongue, like a keen sword, he drove into the eagle's fat with its blade.
sentences.s508-s42: When the emperor of China heard these words, he conceived another plan.
sentences.s508-s43: He ordered that a feast be prepared, and they wished (to do it); they adorned the iwan in the garden.
sentences.s508-s44: They kept drinking until the world grew dark; the wine‑drinkers' heads became transfixed by the wine.
sentences.s508-s45: The commander of China said to the envoy, "May Jupiter be paired with your king."
sentences.s508-s46: When the letter is made clear, we will reply; we will set an auspicious day to meet you.
sentences.s508-s47: Alexander came, a roundel in his hand, half‑drunk from the iwan of the commander of China.
sentences.s508-s48: When the sun lifted its head from the tower of the Lion, the heavens brought the night down beneath.
sentences.s508-s29: We will keep for you this very crown and throne, and may no harm come to you from fate.
sentences.s508-s30: And if it should happen that you come forward from the land to appear before your king.
sentences.s508-s31: Of such things as may contain trinkets in their folds — of golden items and of a steed, a sword, and a gem.
sentences.s508-s32: Also from garments and curtains and an ivory throne, and from rich brocade and a necklace and crown.
sentences.s508-s89: That night and at dawn he sat down calmly upon the king's throne.
sentences.s508-s90: He gave the messenger something and said that, with you, the soul is paired with the Messiah.
sentences.s508-s91: Go before the faghfur and speak Chinese, saying that by coming near to us you have gained reputation.
sentences.s508-s92: If you remain here, China is yours; but if (you go to) another place, strutting proudly is fitting.
sentences.s508-s9: When news reached the faghfur that a messenger had come toward China.
sentences.s508-s10: He received them and sent out some troops; Alexander's boar‑like forces came upon the road.
sentences.s508-s11: When he came to that great palace, he saw that chosen great army.
sentences.s508-s12: He came from the corridor up to his presence; that thoughtful, life‑minded ill‑wisher (the malicious man) stood before him.
sentences.s508-s69: He said to his heart: from now on no one in the world will see me — I have gone away to a hidden place.
sentences.s508-s70: He came from the iwan and sat in his place; meanwhile he shut up the passage so as to prevent a return.
sentences.s508-s71: The exalted faghfur (emperor) opened the treasure; from his giving no grief came into his heart.
sentences.s508-s72: First he ordered fifty jewel-studded crowns and ten ivory thrones.
sentences.s508-s37: Say what you know about his speech, his loftiness, his manliness, and his appearance.
sentences.s508-s38: The messenger said, 'O commander of Chin! Is there anyone on earth like Alexander?'
sentences.s508-s39: In manliness, magnanimity, generosity, and wisdom he surpasses what any person's thought can reach.
sentences.s508-s40: He is loftier than a cypress, and with the strength of an elephant; in generosity his conduct is like the sea of the Nile.
sentences.s508-s85: When the order came before him with the army, he told what had come from his own bazaar.
sentences.s508-s86: His army then cried 'Afarin' (an acclamation); everyone lifted their heads from the ground.
sentences.s508-s87: He perceived that the Chinese was a king; he came on foot, his collar/bosom toward the road.
sentences.s508-s88: Alexander said to him, 'Do not apologize; bring him before the emperor and (speak) these words here in court.'
sentences.s508-s49: Alexander drew near to the emperor; the evil thought left his heart.
sentences.s508-s50: They asked him; he said, “When did you go out last night that you did harm to Mi-gun?”
